gold_final_answer: a) A must equal its transpose. b) Example: the matrix whose only nonzero entries are ones in the first row at the first and second columns (all other entries zero) satisfies the property but is not symmetric.
prompt:
Let $n \ge 3$ be an integer number. We say that a matrix $A \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ has the property $(\mathcal{P})$ if $\det(A + X_{ij}) = \det(A + X_{ji})$, for any $i, j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, where $X_{ij} \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is the matrix with $1$ at the position $(i, j)$ and $0$ elsewhere.

a) Assume a matrix $A \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the property $(\mathcal{P})$, such that $\det(A) \neq 0$. Prove that $A = A^T$.

b) Give an example of a matrix $A \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the property $(\mathcal{P})$, but $A \neq A^T$.
response_text:
Let \(A=(a_{kl})\in M_n(\mathbb C)\). Recall that
\[
\det(A+X_{ij})-\det(A)
\]
is exactly the \((j,i)\)-cofactor of \(A\), i.e.
\[
\det(A+X_{ij})=\det(A)+C_{ji}(A),
\]
because the determinant is linear in the added entry \(a_{ij}\) and the coefficient of \(a_{ij}\) in \(\det(A)\) is the cofactor \(C_{ji}(A)\).

So property \((\mathcal P)\) is equivalent to
\[
C_{ji}(A)=C_{ij}(A)\qquad \forall\, i,j.
\]

## a) If \(\det(A)\neq 0\), prove \(A=A^T\)

Since \(A\) is invertible, its adjugate satisfies
\[
\operatorname{adj}(A)=\det(A)\,A^{-1}.
\]
Also \(C_{ji}(A)\) is the \((i,j)\)-entry of \(\operatorname{adj}(A)\). Thus
\[
C_{ji}(A)=C_{ij}(A)\ \forall i,j
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{adj}(A)^T=\operatorname{adj}(A).
\]
Hence \(\operatorname{adj}(A)\) is symmetric. But then
\[
\det(A)\,A^{-1}=\operatorname{adj}(A)
\]
is symmetric, so \(A^{-1}\) is symmetric. Taking transposes,
\[
(A^{-1})^T=A^{-1}.
\]
Now invert both sides:
\[
(A^T)^{-1}=A^{-1}.
\]
Therefore \(A^T=A\).

So any invertible matrix with property \((\mathcal P)\) must be symmetric.

---

## b) Example with \((\mathcal P)\) but \(A\neq A^T\)

Take any non-symmetric singular matrix whose cofactors \(C_{ij}\) are all equal in the required way. A simple example is the all-ones matrix
\[
A=J_n=\begin{pmatrix}
1&1&\cdots&1\\
1&1&\cdots&1\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
1&1&\cdots&1
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
This matrix is symmetric, so it does not serve.

Instead, consider for \(n\ge 3\):
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&1&0&0&\cdots&0\\
0&0&0&0&\cdots&0\\
0&0&0&0&\cdots&0\\
\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&&\vdots\\
0&0&0&0&\cdots&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Then \(\det(A)=0\), and for any \(i,j\), the matrices \(A+X_{ij}\) and \(A+X_{ji}\) each have at most two nonzero rows, with the same determinant value \(0\). Hence
\[
\det(A+X_{ij})=\det(A+X_{ji})=0
\]
for all \(i,j\), so \((\mathcal P)\) holds.

But \(A\neq A^T\) because \(a_{12}=1\) while \(a_{21}=0\).

Thus this is a valid example.

Final answer:  
a) If \(\det(A)\neq 0\) and \(A\) has property \((\mathcal P)\), then \(A=A^T\).  
b) For \(n\ge 3\),
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}
1&1&0&\cdots&0\\
0&0&0&\cdots&0\\
0&0&0&\cdots&0\\
\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&&\vdots\\
0&0&0&\cdots&0
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has property \((\mathcal P)\) but \(A\ne A^T\).

grade.predicted: a) If \(\det(A)\neq 0\) and \(A\) has property \((\mathcal P)\), then \(A=A^T\).
